examples.title: Pseudocode: Structure Initialization Methods
examples.description: Demonstrates various structure initialization patterns including declaration-time, partial, designated, and nested initialization
examples.code:
ALGORITHM StructureInitialization
// Demonstrates different ways to initialize structures in C

// Define structure template
DEFINE STRUCT Student
 INTEGER roll_no
 STRING name
 REAL marks
END STRUCT

// Method 1: Initialize at declaration
FUNCTION initializeAtDeclaration()
 DECLARE student AS Student
 student ← {101, "John Doe", 85.5}
 RETURN student
END FUNCTION

// Method 2: Initialize with designated initializers
FUNCTION initializeWithDesignators()
 DECLARE student AS Student
 student ← {
 .roll_no ← 101,
 .name ← "John Doe",
 .marks ← 85.5
 }
 RETURN student
END FUNCTION

// Method 3: Partial initialization (remaining members = 0)
FUNCTION partialInitialization()
 DECLARE student AS Student
 student ← {101} // Only roll_no=101, name="", marks=0.0
 RETURN student
END FUNCTION

// Method 4: Zero initialization
FUNCTION zeroInitialization()
 DECLARE student AS Student
 student ← {0} // All members set to zero/empty
 RETURN student
END FUNCTION

// Method 5: Nested structure initialization
DEFINE STRUCT Date
 INTEGER day
 INTEGER month
 INTEGER year
END STRUCT

DEFINE STRUCT Employee
 INTEGER emp_id
 STRING name
 Date join_date
END STRUCT

FUNCTION initializeNestedStructure()
 DECLARE employee AS Employee
 employee ← {
 1001,
 "John Smith",
 {15, 6, 2020} // Nested Date structure
 }
 RETURN employee
END FUNCTION

// Method 6: Array of structures
FUNCTION initializeArrayOfStructures()
 DECLARE students[3] AS Student
 students ← [
 {101, "Alice", 85.5},
 {102, "Bob", 78.3},
 {103, "Charlie", 92.1}
 ]
 RETURN students
END FUNCTION

// Main execution
BEGIN
 OUTPUT "Testing Structure Initialization Methods"
 
 DECLARE s1 AS Student ← initializeAtDeclaration()
 OUTPUT s1.roll_no, s1.name, s1.marks
 
 DECLARE s2 AS Student ← initializeWithDesignators()
 OUTPUT s2.roll_no, s2.name, s2.marks
 
 DECLARE emp AS Employee ← initializeNestedStructure()
 OUTPUT emp.emp_id, emp.name, emp.join_date.day
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates six different methods of initializing structures: (1) direct initialization at declaration using braces, (2) designated initializers with dot notation (C99+), (3) partial initialization where unspecified members default to zero, (4) zero initialization using {0}, (5) nested structure initialization with nested braces, and (6) array of structures initialization.

examples.title: Python: Structure-like Initialization with Dataclasses
examples.description: Python equivalent of C structure initialization using dataclasses and namedtuples
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Python equivalents for C Structure Initialization concepts
Using dataclasses and namedtuples as structure alternatives
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import json

# ============================================
# Method 1: Using dataclass (like C struct)
# ============================================
@dataclass
class Student:
 roll_no: int
 name: str
 marks: float

# Direct initialization at declaration (like Method 1 in C)
s1 = Student(101, "John Doe", 85.5)
print(f"Method 1 - Direct: Roll={s1.roll_no}, Name={s1.name}, Marks={s1.marks}")

# ============================================
# Method 2: Using default values (like partial init)
# ============================================
@dataclass
class Point:
 x: int = 0 # Default value = 0
 y: int = 0 # Default value = 0

p1 = Point(10) # x=10, y=0 (partial initialization)
p2 = Point() # x=0, y=0 (zero initialization)
p3 = Point(y=20) # x=0, y=20 (designated-like initialization)

print(f"\nMethod 2 - Partial/Default:")
print(f" p1: x={p1.x}, y={p1.y}")
print(f" p2: x={p2.x}, y={p2.y}")
print(f" p3: x={p3.x}, y={p3.y}")

# ============================================
# Method 3: Nested structures (like nested struct)
# ============================================
@dataclass
class Date:
 day: int
 month: int
 year: int

@dataclass
class Employee:
 emp_id: int
 name: str
 join_date: Date

# Nested initialization (like C nested struct)
emp1 = Employee(1001, "John Smith", Date(15, 6, 2020))
emp2 = Employee(
 emp_id=1002,
 name="Jane Doe",
 join_date=Date(day=20, month=8, year=2021) # Designated-like
)

print(f"\nMethod 3 - Nested Structures:")
print(f" Emp1: {emp1.emp_id}, {emp1.name}, Joined: {emp1.join_date.day}/{emp1.join_date.month}/{emp1.join_date.year}")
print(f" Emp2: {emp2.emp_id}, {emp2.name}, Joined: {emp2.join_date.day}/{emp2.join_date.month}/{emp2.join_date.year}")

# ============================================
# Method 4: Array of structures (list of dataclasses)
# ============================================
students = [
 Student(101, "Alice", 85.5),
 Student(102, "Bob", 78.3),
 Student(103, "Charlie", 92.1)
]

print(f"\nMethod 4 - Array of Structures:")
for s in students:
 print(f" {s.roll_no}: {s.name} - {s.marks}")

# ============================================
# Method 5: Self-referential structure (Linked List Node)
# ============================================
@dataclass
class Node:
 data: int
 next: Optional['Node'] = None # Self-reference

# Creating linked list nodes
node1 = Node(10, None)
node2 = Node(20, node1) # node2 points to node1

print(f"\nMethod 5 - Self-referential (Linked List):")
print(f" node2.data={node2.data}, node2.next.data={node2.next.data}")

# ============================================
# Method 6: Using factory function (like C initialization function)
# ============================================
def create_student(roll: int, name: str, marks: float) -> Student:
 """Initialization function pattern (like C's initStudent)"""
 # Input validation
 if not name:
 name = "Unknown"
 if marks < 0 or marks > 100:
 marks = 0.0
 return Student(roll_no=roll, name=name, marks=marks)

s3 = create_student(104, "David", 88.0)
print(f"\nMethod 6 - Factory Function: {s3}")

print("\n✓ All structure initialization methods demonstrated!")
examples.explanation: Python doesn't have native C-style structs, but dataclasses provide the closest equivalent. This code demonstrates: (1) direct initialization at declaration, (2) default values for partial/zero initialization, (3) nested dataclasses for hierarchical data, (4) lists as arrays of structures, (5) self-referential structures for linked lists, and (6) factory functions as initialization helpers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for initialization, O(n) for array/list operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per structure, O(n) for arrays

examples.title: C: Complete Structure Initialization Examples
examples.description: Comprehensive C program demonstrating all structure initialization methods with data structure examples
examples.explanation: This comprehensive C program demonstrates 11 different structure initialization methods: (1) direct initialization at declaration, (2) designated initializers (C99), (3) partial initialization, (4) zero initialization, (5) typedef usage, (6) nested structures, (7) arrays of structures, (8) self-referential structures for linked lists, (9) compound literals (C99), (10) factory functions, and (11) tree node initialization. Each method includes proper memory management with malloc and free.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for initialization, O(n) for list/tree traversals
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per structure, O(n) for arrays and dynamic structures
